extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance that prevents landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The ordinance, which will fine landlords $500 for the first offense and $2,500 for subsequent offenses, was introduced in September and drew comments from over 20 tenants. According to Sami Saeed, a public policy major at the University of Maryland, 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people. … That is the situation renters are faced with. That is the situation I was faced with. It happened to me.'

organizations: College Park’s Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC)
extraction.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee, the College Park’s Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC), resubmitted a year-old report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council in January, recommending upgrades to protect elementary school children. The report graded 75 bus stops and identified seven that scored low on safety, with recommendations including adding bike lanes and installing a sidewalk. As committee chairman Donald Hays said, 'I personally feel that is an outrage and there has been very little movement and very little progress,' particularly at the bus stop at St. Andrews Place and Duke Street, which he believes 'continues to be the most dangerous place for elementary school children to wait for their bus that could be fixed tomorrow.'

title.rendered: City considers expanding permit parking

excerpt.rendered: College Park City Council members in January asked city staff to conduct a study to determine whether to restrict parking in residential neighborhoods by issuing permits. Some neighborhoods, including those with crowded streets and few driveways in North College Park and around the University of Maryland campus, already have permit-only parking. The study would examine […]

extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking in residential neighborhoods, with a study to determine whether to restrict parking on additional streets. Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller proposed the study, suggesting that permit-only parking could help address overcrowding in homes by limiting permits to homeowners and tenants whose names appear on a lease, saying 'Where are they going to park their car now, because they are not on a lease agreement, they are not listed as [the] owner-occupied property owner.' The council discussed the proposal, with former Councilmember Susan Whitney expressing concerns that paid street parking could lead to less green space and increased stormwater runoff, and suggesting the city offer incentives to encourage residents to own fewer cars, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that.'

extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend that Prince George’s County amend a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build a four-story, 87-unit apartment building for older residents on Branchville Road. extraction.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a bill to change the state's zoning law, allowing for more dense housing construction, which has sparked division among state officials representing College Park. House Speaker Joseline Peña-Melnyk supports the proposal, stating 'Maryland has a housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes... When people can’t afford to live here, we lose workers. We lose families. And we lose the economic growth that allows us to fund schools, transportation and public safety.' In contrast, State Senator Jim Rosapepe and Prince George’s County Councilmember Tom Dernoga have expressed concerns about the proposal, with Dernoga calling it a 'sprawl multiplier' that will worsen congestion and strain public safety resources.

As speaker, Peña-Melnyk aims to collaborate with both Democrats and Republicans, calling herself an 'inclusive' leader.

extraction.ai_summary: The Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project in College Park is moving forward after over a decade, with contractor selection underway and work expected to begin by mid-summer. As Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson said, 'It’s unfortunate that this project took this long, but I’m glad that we’re finally at the stage where it [went] to bid,' and 'The sooner we get construction started, the sooner it will finish.' It’s going to bring us up to the current standard, but not up to current climate.'
